g.data-narration: This topic covers methods to restore images that have been degraded by various types of noise. We will learn how to recover original images using mathematical filters and estimation techniques.
g.text: Image Restoration in the Presence of Noise Only

g.data-narration: We examine different noise types: Gaussian noise follows a normal distribution while additive noise is simply added to pixel values. Understanding noise characteristics helps choose the right restoration method.

The mathematical model represents the degraded image as the original image plus noise. The noise component follows a Gaussian distribution with zero mean and specific variance representing intensity fluctuations.
g.text: I(x) = f(x) + g(x) g(x) ~ N(0, σ²)
Observed = Original + Noise Noise has Gaussian distribution

g.data-narration: The Wiener filter is a classic restoration method that minimizes mean squared error. It uses frequency domain processing to separate signal from noise based on their power spectra ratios.
g.text: Minimizes MSE between restored and original
Adds regularization to prevent noise amplification

g.data-narration: Maximum A Posteriori estimation uses Bayes theorem to find the most likely original image. It combines prior knowledge about image properties with observed noisy data for reconstruction.
g.text: p(y|I) ∝ p(I|y) p(y) Î = argmaxₙ p(I|y)
Maximizes posterior probability given observed image

g.data-narration: Wiener filter offers simplicity and speed but assumes Gaussian noise. MAP estimation provides better results but requires more computation. Both methods are fundamental to image restoration.
